critic_findings.message: Line 117 (null pullRequest node path inside fetchPREnrichmentGraphQL) is not covered. Add a test that stubs a response with pullRequest: null and asserts { ci: no_ci, reviewDetails: no_reviews }.
Line 136 (classifyError Error-with-no-status branch) is not covered. Add a test that throws plain new Error('msg') with no .status property and asserts reviewError === 'msg'.
Line 137 (classifyError 'unknown' fallback) is not covered. Add a test that throws a non-Error, non-object value (e.g., throw null) and asserts reviewError === 'unknown'.

Line 172 (mapRollupState unknown-state fallback returning 'no_ci') is not covered. Add a test with a rollup state not in SUCCESS/FAILURE/ERROR/PENDING/EXPECTED (e.g., 'STALE') and assert ci.status === 'no_ci'.

findings_details.title: makeGitHubClient double-wraps GraphQL responses — mock shape is wrong
findings_details.body: The makeGitHubClient helper (line 51-59) wraps every requestImpl return value as { status: 200, data: result, headers: {} }. The GraphQL route handlers in this file return a full { status, headers, data: { data: { repository: ... } } } shaped object, which gets wrapped again. doGraphQL reads response.data as the GraphQLResponseEnvelope, so the envelope seen by fetchPREnrichmentGraphQL is { status: 200, headers: {...}, data: { data: { repository: ... } } } — not { data: { repository: ... } }. response.data?.data?.repository evaluates to undefined in all three tests that use makeGitHubClient with a GraphQL route (dedupePRs, author-agnostic, SHA-change). Those tests pass only because they do not assert on enrichment data (CI status, review state), giving false assurance that the enrichment path works. Fix: have the GraphQL handler return the raw GraphQLResponseEnvelope { data: { repository: ... } } without the status/headers wrapper, since makeGitHubClient provides those.

findings_details.title: collectFailedCheckNames silently ignores failed StatusContext nodes
findings_details.body: The PR_ENRICHMENT_QUERY fetches both StatusContext (with state and context fields) and CheckRun nodes. The ContextNode union type includes StatusContext at line 94. However collectFailedCheckNames (lines 158-165) only appends names for __typename === 'CheckRun' nodes — it never inspects StatusContext nodes. A legacy status check that reports FAILURE or ERROR state will not appear in failedChecks. The rollup-level state will still be FAILURE/ERROR so ci.status will be correct, but failedChecks will be incomplete, causing the UI to show no failed check names for status-API failures. Fix: add a branch for StatusContext with state in FAILURE_ROLLUP_STATES, appending node.context as the name.

findings_details.title: isBot re-export is never imported from this module — dead public API
findings_details.body: Line 198 re-exports isBot from github-pr-transform.ts with the comment 'Re-exported for tests that want to assert isBot wiring directly.' No test file imports isBot from github-pr-fetch-graphql.ts — the isBot tests in github-pr-transform.test.ts import directly from github-pr-transform.ts (confirmed by grep). The re-export adds an unintended public surface to github-pr-fetch-graphql.ts, whose responsibility is PR enrichment, not bot detection. Fix: remove the export { isBot } line and its comment.

findings_details.title: request() and graphql() duplicate the rate-limit/budget/inflight preamble
findings_details.body: The 12-line preamble — circuit check, budget check, inflight-dedup lookup, promise registration, inflight cleanup — is structurally identical in request() (lines 112-127) and graphql() (lines 142-157). The only differences are the cache-key function (cacheKey vs graphqlCacheKey) and the dispatch function (doRequest vs doGraphQL). This violates DRY and means any change to the gating policy (e.g. adding a third gate) must be made in two places. Fix: extract a private executeWithGates<T>(key: string, dispatch: () => Promise<GitHubRequestResult<T>>, consumer?: BudgetConsumer) helper that owns the check-budget + check-circuit + inflight pattern, then have both public methods call it.

findings_details.title: Team requestedReviewer login is undefined at runtime — null check insufficient
findings_details.body: The GraphQL query spreads only 'User' and 'Bot' inline fragments on requestedReviewer. When a Team is a requested reviewer, the response object has __typename='Team' but no login field (Teams have 'name', not 'login'). The filter at line 187 only removes null values, so Team objects pass through with login=undefined. This undefined value flows into buildReviewerMap and the requestedReviewers array, potentially causing string operations on undefined downstream. Fix: add a type check in the filter to only pass through objects where __typename is 'User' or 'Bot' (both have login), or add a 'Team' branch to the fragment with ... on Team { slug } and handle it in the mapping. Add a test case for Team requestedReviewer.

findings_details.title: Three exported interfaces are never imported by any consumer
findings_details.body: CapturedWarn (line 11), GraphQLResponse (line 29), and StubbedGraphQLClient (line 35) are all exported but the sole consumer (github-pr-fetch-graphql.test.ts) imports only the three functions by name and never references these interfaces by name. No other file imports the helpers module. These are dead exports that violate the no-dead-code rule.

findings_details.title: warn-once test never asserts that log.warn was called or that dedup skipped a second call
findings_details.body: The test named 'warns once per unknown requestedReviewer __typename and drops it from tracking' asserts only (a) requestedReviewers equals ['carol'] — which tests the filter path, not the warn path — and (b) typeof warnUnknownReviewerTypenames === 'function' — which tests nothing. The warn call itself (log.warn) is never spied on or asserted. The second invocation of warnUnknownReviewerTypenames at line 200-202 asserts nothing about the result. Deleting the warnedReviewerTypenames.add(tn) line in the source would not break this test. Fix: spy on log.warn (via vi.spyOn on the logger) before the call, then assert it was called exactly once with the correct event/typename, and that the second invocation does NOT call it again.

findings_details.title: Plain allocator object fails structural check against RateBudgetAllocator class
findings_details.body: Lines 110-114 and 122-126 pass { checkBudget: vi.fn(), recordUsage: vi.fn() } as budgetAllocator. RateBudgetAllocator is a class with private fields (allocations, usage, totalBudget, windowStart, now). TypeScript structural typing requires those private fields to match — a plain object cannot satisfy a class type with privates. Fix: extract a minimal IBudgetAllocator interface with only the two public methods (checkBudget, recordUsage), use that interface as the parameter type on GitHubClientOptions.budgetAllocator instead of the concrete class.

findings_details.title: Missing required login field in test ReviewerNode stub
findings_details.body: At line 152, warnUnknownReviewerTypenames is called with [{ requestedReviewer: { __typename: 'Mannequin' } }]. The parameter type is PullRequestNode['reviewRequests']['nodes'] which resolves to Array<{ requestedReviewer: AuthorNode | null }>. AuthorNode is defined as { __typename: string; login: string } — login is required. The plain object is missing login, causing TS2741. Fix: add login: '' to the stub object to satisfy the type.

findings_details.title: Unknown requestedReviewer __typename silently dropped with no log warning
findings_details.body: The allowlist filter (User|Bot) silently discards any requestedReviewer node whose __typename is not User or Bot (e.g. Mannequin, EnterpriseUserAccount, or a future GitHub schema addition). Currently that matches REST behavior and is intentional for Team. However there is no log warning for unrecognized subtypes. If GitHub adds a new requestedReviewer subtype, reviewer-tracking degrades silently with no observable signal in logs. A one-time (per-session or per-typename) warn log when __typename is not in the known-good set plus not in the known-ignore set (Team) would allow operators to detect schema drift. Severity is low because the failure mode is silent omission of a reviewer, not data corruption or security impact.

findings_details.title: Test file exceeds 300-line limit (358 lines)
findings_details.body: The file is 358 lines, 58 lines over the 300-line hard limit. The Round 3 additions (installCapturingLogger helper + 4 warn-capturing test cases = ~55 lines) pushed it over. The gate confirms this as a fail. The file must be split or trimmed, or an allow-list entry added with justification.

findings_details.title: Test file exceeds 300-line limit (308 lines)
findings_details.body: The file is 308 lines, 8 lines over the limit. This branch modified the file in commits 0500e00 and 21b957a. The gate confirms this as a fail. Must be split or trimmed, or an allow-list entry added with justification.

findings_details.title: BudgetAllocatorLike placement inconsistent with OctokitLike pattern
findings_details.body: OctokitLike is defined co-located with its consumer in github-client.ts (line 64). BudgetAllocatorLike is defined co-located with its implementor in rate-budget-allocator.ts (line 41) and imported into github-client.ts. The two Like-suffix structural interfaces follow opposite placement conventions. There is no functional impact — TypeScript structural typing means test stubs satisfy BudgetAllocatorLike without importing it, and tsc passes. The inconsistency is navigational: a reader expecting the Like-pattern to live with its consumer will not find BudgetAllocatorLike in github-client.ts. The co-types argument (BudgetDecision, BudgetConsumer) favors the current placement, but the established project pattern favors github-client.ts. Fix: either document the placement rationale in a comment on BudgetAllocatorLike, or move it to github-client.ts alongside OctokitLike.
